content.text: The Vendor Relationship as an Operational Dependency

When a GenAI service depends on an external LLM API, the LLM provider becomes an operational dependency with its own reliability characteristics, support processes, and contractual SLAs. Managing this dependency is a distinct operational skill — it is not the same as managing an internal service outage, and the runbooks are different.

Provider incidents require a parallel response track: internal mitigation (fallback models, request queuing, circuit breakers) runs simultaneously with provider escalation (support tickets, TAM notifications, status page monitoring). The two tracks are independent because the provider cannot fix your service faster by you waiting for them — you must always pursue internal mitigation first, regardless of whether the provider eventually provides relief.

attrs.alt: LLM provider escalation decision flow: detect anomaly, check internal causes first, check provider status page, activate fallback, open ticket if needed, document for SLA claim

content.text: The First Rule: Check Internal Causes Before Escalating

Before contacting a provider, rule out internal causes. A deploy 20 minutes before the latency spike, a prompt change in the config repo, or a model version change in the configuration are all internal root causes that look indistinguishable from a provider incident in the first 5 minutes. Escalating to the provider for an internally-caused incident wastes the on-call engineer's attention and erodes your credibility with the provider support team.
Check the deploy log: any deploy in the 30 minutes before the anomaly is a suspect
Check prompt/config changes: any config management commit in the last hour
Check the model version configuration: did the pinned version change, or was an unpinned version silently updated by the provider
Check traffic pattern: sudden traffic spike can cause latency without provider degradation
Only after ruling these out: check the provider status page
Reading the Provider Status Page

All major LLM providers expose a public status page using StatusPage.io or equivalent. The status page has two levels of signal: the current operational status (which updates every few minutes) and the active incidents list (which provides incident timeline and impact scope).
Status Page Indicator
Meaning
Action
Operational (green)
No known issues
Check internal causes more carefully; open support ticket if issue persists
Degraded Performance
Some users or features affected
Check if your usage pattern matches the affected scope; monitor for update
Partial Outage
Significant fraction of requests failing
Activate fallback immediately; subscribe to status page updates
Major Outage
Service broadly unavailable
Activate fallback; open P1 support ticket; notify internal stakeholders
Under Maintenance
Scheduled window
Check if maintenance window was communicated in advance; may still need fallback

Do not rely on the status page alone. Providers have been known to show 'Operational' on their status page while customers experience significant degradation, especially for new or regional issues. If your service is degraded and the status page shows green after 10 minutes, open a support ticket regardless.
Automated Status Page Monitoring

Do not ask the on-call engineer to manually check provider status pages during an incident. Run a status monitor that polls both providers every 60 seconds and posts to Slack when new incidents appear or existing ones change state.

# provider_status_monitor.py — polls LLM provider status pages
import os, time, requests
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone

SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ['SLACK_ONCALL_WEBHOOK']

@dataclass
class ProviderIncident:
    provider: str
    incident_id: str
    name: str
    status: str         # 'investigating' | 'identified' | 'monitoring' | 'resolved'
    impact: str         # 'none' | 'minor' | 'major' | 'critical'
    created_at: str
    updated_at: str

def fetch_active_incidents(provider: str, status_api_url: str) -> list[ProviderIncident]:
    """Fetches unresolved incidents from the provider's status page API."""
    resp = requests.get(
        f'{status_api_url}/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json',
        timeout=10,
    )
    incidents = []
    for inc in resp.json().get('incidents', []):
        incidents.append(ProviderIncident(
            provider=provider,
            incident_id=inc['id'],
            name=inc['name'],
            status=inc['status'],
            impact=inc['impact'],
            created_at=inc['created_at'],
            updated_at=inc['updated_at'],
        ))
    return incidents

def post_provider_incident_to_slack(
    incident: ProviderIncident,
    is_new: bool,
) -> None:
    action = 'NEW' if is_new else 'UPDATED'
    impact_emoji = {
        'critical': ':rotating_light:',
        'major':    ':no_entry:',
        'minor':    ':warning:',
        'none':     ':white_check_mark:',
    }.get(incident.impact, ':grey_question:')

    message = (
        f'{impact_emoji} *[{action}] Provider Incident: {incident.provider}*\n'
        f'  Name: {incident.name}\n'
        f'  Status: {incident.status} | Impact: {incident.impact}\n'
        f'  Started: {incident.created_at}\n'
        f'  Last update: {incident.updated_at}'
    )
    requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={'text': message}, timeout=5)

PROVIDERS = {
    'openai':    'https://status.openai.com',
    'anthropic': 'https://status.anthropic.com',
}

def monitor_loop(poll_interval_seconds: int = 60) -> None:
    """Polls provider status pages and notifies on new or changed incidents."""
    seen: dict[str, str] = {}  # incident_id -> last seen status

    while True:
        for provider, url in PROVIDERS.items():
            try:
                incidents = fetch_active_incidents(provider, url)
                for inc in incidents:
                    key = f'{provider}:{inc.incident_id}'
                    is_new = key not in seen
                    changed = seen.get(key) != inc.status
                    if is_new or changed:
                        post_provider_incident_to_slack(inc, is_new)
                        seen[key] = inc.status
            except Exception as e:
                print(f'Error checking {provider}: {e}')
        time.sleep(poll_interval_seconds)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    monitor_loop()

Deploy this as a sidecar process or Lambda function that runs continuously. The seen dictionary prevents duplicate notifications for the same incident. When an incident is resolved by the provider, the resolved status triggers one final Slack notification and the key is dropped from the seen dict on the next cycle.
Opening a Provider Support Ticket

When to open a ticket: the provider status page does not list the issue after 10 minutes of degradation, or you need an SLA-backed resolution commitment, or the degradation affects safety/quality metrics in ways that require investigation beyond what the status page provides.

# open_provider_ticket.py — opens a P1 support ticket with incident context
import os, requests
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def open_provider_support_ticket(
    provider: str,        # 'openai' | 'anthropic'
    subject: str,
    description: str,
    internal_incident_id: str,
    metrics_snapshot: dict,
) -> str:
    """Creates a P1 support ticket and returns the ticket URL."""
    # Each provider uses a different support API format;
    # this shows the generic pattern used via Zendesk-backed portals.
    support_url = os.environ[f'{provider.upper()}_SUPPORT_API_URL']
    api_token   = os.environ[f'{provider.upper()}_SUPPORT_API_TOKEN']

    body = (
        f'Internal incident ID: {internal_incident_id}\n'
        f'Detected at: {datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()}\n\n'
        f'Description:\n{description}\n\n'
        f'Metrics at time of incident:\n'
        + '\n'.join(f'  {k}: {v}' for k, v in metrics_snapshot.items())
        + '\n\nWe are investigating internally in parallel. '
        'Please confirm whether you have an active incident '
        'affecting API availability or quality.'
    )

    payload = {
        'request': {
            'subject': f'[P1] {subject}',
            'comment': {'body': body},
            'priority': 'urgent',
            'custom_fields': [
                {'id': 'impact_level', 'value': 'production_outage'},
            ],
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        f'{support_url}/api/v2/requests',
        json=payload,
        headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_token}'},
        timeout=15,
    )
    ticket_id = resp.json()['request']['id']
    return f'{support_url}/requests/{ticket_id}'

The ticket body should include the internal incident ID, exact timestamps, metrics at the time of detection, and a clear statement of what you are observing — not a theory about root cause. Including your metrics snapshot (error rate, latency P95, request volume) lets the provider support team correlate your report with their internal signals immediately.
TAM (Technical Account Manager) Escalation
Enterprise contracts with LLM providers typically include a TAM — a named contact who provides faster access to engineering teams than the general support queue. TAM escalation is appropriate for:
P1 incidents where the provider has not acknowledged the status page issue within 30 minutes
Quality regressions where the model behavior changed without a status page notice (often model version updates)
Safety regressions where the provider must investigate whether a model change caused harmful output distributions
Recurring issues that require a deeper conversation about your usage pattern vs. their infrastructure
Escalation Method
Response Time Expectation
Use When
Support ticket (self-serve)
Hours to 1 business day
Non-urgent investigation; minor degradation; SLA documentation
TAM email/Slack
30 min–2h (business hours)
Unacknowledged P1 after 30 min; quality/safety regression; recurring issue
TAM emergency phone
Complete API outage with no status page acknowledgment; safety incident requiring provider investigation
Escalation to provider engineering (via TAM)
Hours
Root cause investigation after incident resolves; architectural changes needed
SLA Credit Tracking

Provider SLAs define the uptime commitment and the credit mechanism for breaches. Most LLM API SLAs are 99.9% or 99.95% availability, which corresponds to 43.8 or 21.9 minutes of allowed downtime per month. When a provider incident exceeds this, you are entitled to a service credit — but credits must typically be requested within 30–60 days of the incident.

# sla_credit_claim.py — tracks provider SLA adherence and drafts credit claims
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

@dataclass
class ProviderSLARecord:
    provider: str
    incident_start: datetime
    incident_end: datetime
    our_impact_start: datetime   # when we first detected impact
    our_impact_end: datetime     # when our service recovered
    sla_commitment: float        # e.g. 0.999 for 99.9%

    @property
    def downtime_minutes(self) -> float:
        delta = self.incident_end - self.incident_start
        return delta.total_seconds() / 60

    @property
    def monthly_budget_minutes(self) -> float:
        """Minutes of allowed downtime per 30-day window."""
        return (1 - self.sla_commitment) * 30 * 24 * 60

    @property
    def sla_breached(self) -> bool:
        return self.downtime_minutes > self.monthly_budget_minutes

    def draft_credit_claim_email(self) -> str:
        return (
            f'Subject: SLA Credit Claim — Incident {self.incident_start.date()}\n\n'
            f'Dear {self.provider} Support Team,\n\n'
            f'We experienced service impact between '
            f'{self.our_impact_start.isoformat()} and '
            f'{self.our_impact_end.isoformat()} UTC '
            f'({self.downtime_minutes:.0f} minutes) '
            f'attributable to your API incident.\n\n'
            f'Our contracted SLA is {self.sla_commitment:.1%} '
            f'({self.monthly_budget_minutes:.1f} minutes/month allowed downtime). '
            f'This incident consumed {self.downtime_minutes:.0f} minutes, '
            f'{"exceeding" if self.sla_breached else "within"} our monthly budget.\n\n'
            'We request a credit in accordance with the API SLA agreement. '
            'Please reference the attached incident log and internal ticket ID.\n\n'
            'Best regards,\n[Engineering Team]'
        )

if __name__ == '__main__':
    record = ProviderSLARecord(
        provider='OpenAI',
        incident_start=datetime(2026, 6, 15, 14, 0),
        incident_end=datetime(2026, 6, 15, 15, 30),
        our_impact_start=datetime(2026, 6, 15, 14, 5),
        our_impact_end=datetime(2026, 6, 15, 15, 32),
        sla_commitment=0.999,
    )
    print(f'SLA breached: {record.sla_breached}')
    print(f'Monthly budget: {record.monthly_budget_minutes:.1f} min')
    print(f'Incident duration: {record.downtime_minutes:.0f} min')
    print()
    print(record.draft_credit_claim_email())

Track provider incidents in a structured log with start time, end time, and impact on your service. This log is the evidence base for SLA credit claims. Without it, you have no documentation of how long the incident lasted from your perspective, which the provider will use to minimize the credit calculation.
Multi-Provider Strategy: When to Switch

If you have contracts with multiple LLM providers, provider escalation includes the decision of whether to switch primary providers for the duration of the incident. This is a higher-stakes version of activating a fallback model.
Scenario
Recommendation
Provider P1 with fallback available and tested
Activate fallback immediately; do not wait for provider resolution
Provider P1 with untested fallback
Activate fallback with reduced traffic first; validate quality before full switch
Provider quality regression (not availability)
Do not switch unless eval quality on fallback has been measured; quality may vary between providers
Safety regression attributed to provider model update
Pin to last-known-good version first; escalate to TAM; do not switch provider until root cause is clear
Test fallback providers regularly in non-production environments — a fallback that has not been tested recently may have its own quality or API compatibility issues
Maintain a comparison eval for each provider you use so you can assess quality trade-offs before switching during an incident
Document the switch decision in the incident ticket: when you switched, what traffic percentage, and what eval quality you observed on the fallback
Summary

Vendor escalation for LLM providers follows a structured flow: rule out internal causes first, check the status page, activate fallback in parallel with escalation, and open a support ticket if the issue is unacknowledged after 10 minutes. Automate status page monitoring so the on-call engineer is notified of provider incidents without manual polling. Maintain TAM contacts with clear thresholds for when to use them. Track every provider incident with timestamps and impact metrics to support SLA credit claims. If you operate a multi-provider strategy, pre-validate fallback quality with comparative evals so that switching providers during an incident is a documented, tested procedure rather than an improvised one.
